content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 17,
1992
Amelia Ann Albano
Deputy City Attorney
City of San Buenaventura
501 Poli Street
Post Office Box 99
Ventura,
CA
93002-0099
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-92-191
Dear Ms. Albano:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of City Councilmember James L.
Monahan and an unidentified
city councilmember regarding their responsibilities under the
conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”) •1.
QUESTIONS
1.
May Councilmember Monahan avoid disqualification from
governmental decisions by foregoing “promised” income to which he
otherwise would be entitled?
2.
May a city councilmember return income received from a
client so that he or she can participate in a matter concerning
that source of income?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
Councilmember Monahan may participate in decisions
affecting the -park owner by refusing to accept compensation from
the park owner.
This conclusion assumes that Councilmember
Monahan has received no other payments of $250 or more from the
park owner during the
12 months preceding the decision.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 80? • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 •
(916)322-5660


Our File No.
A-92-191
Page
2
2.
A councilmeinber who receives compensation for his or her
services in the amount of $250 or more prior to a city council
decision may not participate in any council decision which will
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his or
her source of income.
FACTS
On February 12,
1992, the Ventura River overran its banks due
to heavy rains,
and flooded the Ventura Beach Recreational Vehicle
Park
(the “park”)
located in the City of Ventura.
In response to the emergency,
Councilmember Monahan went to
the park to see if he could be of assistance.
The councilmember
also arranged to have a backhoe and operator from his wholly—owned
business, American Hot Tap Service,
and a dinghy brought to the
park.
At the park, the backhoe was used to help remove two
recreational vehicles from the park and the dinghywas used for
evacuation purposes.
Once the flood waters subsided, the backhoe
was used to clear out the mud and to assist people with removing
their recreational vehicles.
On the day of the flood, the councilmember told the park
owner that he would send him a bill for the work being done.
On
February 20th, the councilinember’s business billed the park owner,
in the normal course of business,
for the work performed on
February 12th.
On February 25th, the councilmember sent a second
bill for work performed on February 13th.
The two bills totalled
more than $700.
However, because of the nature of the emergenc
services provided on March 17,
1992, the councilmember sent a
third letter to the park cancelling the bill.
Since the flood, the city council was confronted with
questions concerning the reopening of the park and the status and
options available with respect to the park’s permit.
City staff
was directed to provide reports concerning the issues to the city
council.
On March 2,
1992, city staff briefed the city council on
the issues pertaining to the park’s permit and the city council
continued the matter until March 23,
1992.
You stated that the
councilmember has not received income from the park,
was not
promised income when the work was done,
and cancelled any debt
within 30 days of rendering the service.
MALYSIS
The Act’s conflict-of-interest provisions require public
officials to disclose their private economic interests and
disqualify themselves from participating in decisions which could
significantly affect those interests.
(Sections 87100—87500.)
Accordingly,
each year Councilmember Monahan, for example, is
required to disclose his sources of income of $250 or more.
(Sections 87207 and 87302.)
In addition, he is required to
disqualify himself from making, participating in,
or using his
official position to influence any governmental decision in which


Our File No.
A—92—l91
Page
3
he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.)
A public official has a financial interest in a decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally,
on,
among other interests,
any source of income
totaling $250 or more provided to, received by or promised to the
official during the preceding 12 months.
(Section 87103(c).)
According to your facts, the park owner is a source of $250
or more in income “promised to” Councilmember Monahan within the
preceding 12 months.
(Sections 82030(a)
and 87103(c).)
Therefore,
he is required to disqualify himself from participating
in any decision, wh$ch includes any discussions, concerning the
future of the park.
2
Mr. Monahan would like to participate in matters affecting
the park owner which are before the city council.
Thus, he is
willing to forego the income to which otherwise he may have been
entitled by virtue of the work performed by his business.
You ask
if he may avoid disqualification from city council decisions
concerning the park by cancelling his bill and foregoing any
payment for the services rendered to the park owner in connection
with the flood.
We have previously advised that if a public official accepts
payment, the official receives no income for purposes of the Act
if he or she signs over the check to a charitable organization ar.c.
foregoes any income tax deduction for the charitable donation.
(Marshall Advice Letter,
No. A-88-129; Vose Advice Letter,
No.
A-
86-318.)
Thus,
we conclude that Councilmember Monahan may
participate in decisions affecting the park owner by refusing to
accept compensation from the park owner.
This conclusion assumes
that the councilmember has received no other payments of $250 or
more from the park owner during the 12 months preceding the
decision.
In the second situation you presented, the councilmember,
who
is a sole proprietor, would like to return income received for
services previously rendered so that he could participate in a
matthr concerning that source of income.
As indicated above,
we
have previously advised that if an official accepts compensation
and endorses a client’s check directly to a charitable
organization, the official has received no income.
However, if
the official cashes or deposits a client’s check for whom he or
she has rendered services, the official has received compensation
from the client.
There is no authority to conclude that under
2
If Mr.
Monahan or his wholly owned business, American Hot Tap
Service, receives $250 or more in income from the park owner, he
also would be required to disclose that income.


Our File No.
A—92—191
Page
4
such circumstances the official has not received income.
3
Therefore,
we conclude that the councilmember who receives
compensation for his services in the amount of $250 or more prior
to a city council decision may not participate in any council
decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on his source of income.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-590l.
SH: DRS: aa
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting general Counsel
In addition, unlike the gift statute, which expressly excludes
gifts which are returned, donated to a charitable organization, or
reimbursed within 30 days of receipt,
no such statutory authority
exists for income.
(Section 82030(b).)
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1O per page.
Deanne Stone
Counsel,
Legal Division
